rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank. Virginia is absent from the published index entirely and must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt). Calibrated to methodology anchors and to the states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, Alaska 47.5, Colorado 52.5, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0. Virginia at 55.6 sits between Pennsylvania and New York, driven by an unusually strong independent audit function (JLARC) and statutory repair mechanisms, offset by a six-year Marcus Alert implementation failure, a restrictive public records regime, and a custodial-death record handled through litigation rather than acknowledgment. Composite verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions from site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs on {AWR:3.6, EMP:2.8, ACT:3.0, EQU:3.4, BND:2.8, ACC:3.4, SYS:3.6, INT:3.2}, which returned {"composite": 55.6, "band": "Functional", "integrationPremium": 0}. Supporting arithmetic: mean of the 8 raw dimensions 3.2250, base composite 55.63, standard deviation 0.3072 so consistencyMult 1.0, weakDims 8 of 8 below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0. No hypothetical or sensitivity composite is reported in this proposal; every figure above is the computeCompositeFromDimensions output.
attribution_note: Scores Commonwealth of Virginia conduct only. Virginia has the nation's heaviest federal-workforce concentration, so federal action is separated carefully. Medicaid work and community engagement requirements beginning January 2027 derive from 2025 federal law and are NOT scored against Virginia; what is scored is Virginia's implementation discretion - the $3.5M caseworker verification support, the end-of-September-2026 notification timeline, and the January and February 2026 plain-language FAQs. The expiration of enhanced federal ACA subsidies is federal; Virginia's $150M state premium assistance for up to 200,000 people is a state choice and is fully scored. Executive Order 10 (2026-01-17, rescinding the prior mandate that state and local law enforcement enforce federal civil immigration law) and Executive Order 16 (May 2026, barring state property from federal immigration enforcement) are fully scored as state discretion. The DOJ settlement and its successor permanent injunction (entered 2025-01-15) are federal-court-supervised, but Virginia's compliance conduct and continued semi-annual reporting are scored. The threatened loss of at least $42.5M/year in federal homelessness funding is federal; Virginia's failure to substitute an equivalent alternative is scored under B4.

cross_state_consistency_flag.topic: FOIA / public records exemptions shielding the executive and legislature
cross_state_consistency_flag.finding: Virginia's broad 'working papers' exemption shields the Governor's Office and legislators from disclosure; the Virginia Court of Appeals in Commonwealth v. Sawyer (2025-04-29) reversed an order compelling the Office of the Governor to produce records under that exemption. Virginia is one of only two states that has never defined 'personnel records.' With 133 localities there are effectively 133 different records regimes. This is structurally the same defect flagged for Michigan and Massachusetts. Virginia differs in one material respect: its legislature funds JLARC, an independent audit body that publishes findings damaging to the executive, and agencies file mandatory public RD-series reports to the General Assembly, so unflattering performance data reaches the public by a route other than records requests.
cross_state_consistency_flag.scoring_effect: AB3 Transparency held at 3/5 rather than 4/5 because the disclosure regime is genuinely restrictive; not dropped to 2/5 because JLARC and RD-series reporting provide real public access to unflattering performance data. Recommended cross-state rule: when scoring AB3 for a jurisdiction with a broad executive/legislative records exemption, test whether an independently funded body publishes damaging findings about the executive. If yes, cap at 3 rather than floor at 2. Michigan and Massachusetts AB3 scores should be re-checked against this test for consistency.

key_evidence: JLARC, the legislature's independent audit body, found in July 2023 that Virginia school divisions receive roughly 14% less than the national average (about $1,900 per student); Virginia then raised recurring K-12 funding by $1.85B (21.4%) from FY2023 to FY2026, lifted the 2009 support-staff cap, and added $2B more in the 2026 budget - a documented correction following a self-commissioned unflattering audit.

Marcus Alert, enacted 2020, remains materially incomplete six years on: 89 of 133 localities are exempt, only one Community Services Board area has final DBHDS approval, and the statutory deadline now sits at 2028-07-01; the 2026 budget did add $11.4M over the biennium to fund the remaining 13 CSBs.

Virginia committed $150M in state ACA premium assistance for as many as 200,000 people after enhanced federal subsidies expired, plus $3.5M for Medicaid eligibility verification support - a state absorbing cost to offset federal contraction.

Charles Givens died at Marion Correctional Treatment Center on 2022-02-05 and other people there were hospitalized for hypothermia at least 13 times in three years; the Commonwealth litigated three years to an April 2025 defence verdict rather than acknowledging harm, while a Corrections Ombudsman created in 2023-24 has yet to publish findings.

Governor Spanberger vetoed SB 378 / HB 1263 on 2026-05-14, blocking collective bargaining rights for approximately 500,000 public employees after campaigning in support; AFSCME described it as a broken promise and EPI said her amendments would have left workers' rights optional.

Virginia's broad FOIA 'working papers' exemption shielded the Office of the Governor in Commonwealth v. Sawyer (2025-04-29), and Virginia is one of only two states never to have defined 'personnel records.'

Wrongful incarceration compensation is statutory and indexed at $55,000 per year (Va. Code 8.01-195.11), paid to three men in 2025 and to exoneree Messiah Johnson in 2026 - a standing repair mechanism rather than an ad hoc gesture.

The Virginia Opioid Abatement Authority has awarded more than $112M to 176 projects since late 2022, including $32.7M to 81 cities and counties and $14M across 18 state agencies in FY2025, after screening 158 applications.

evidence.claim: state funded the long-unfunded remainder of its own crisis response mandate in the 2026 budget

evidence.quote: So-called 'exempt' localities account for two-thirds of all Virginia localities (89 out of 133)

evidence.quote: Virginia governor breaks promise to public service workers, vetoes collective bargaining bill

evidence.claim: state's response to a death in custody was three years of litigation rather than acknowledgment

evidence.quote: In Commonwealth v. Sawyer, Sawyer sought certain records from the Office of the Governor, which denied her request citing the working papers and correspondence exemption
evidence.claim: broad records exemption shields the executive from disclosure

evidence.source: Virginia DBHDS report to the General Assembly, SD4

evidence.quote: may be awarded compensation of $55,000 per year, adjusted on July 1 of each fiscal year by the year-over-year percentage increase in the Chained Consumer Price Index
evidence.claim: standing statutory repair mechanism for wrongful incarceration, indexed to inflation

evidence.quote: Since the OAA began operations in late 2022, the Authority has awarded more than $112 million in funding to support 176 opioid and substance use abatement projects across the Commonwealth
evidence.claim: sustained upstream root-cause spending routed through an independent statutory authority
evidence.source: Virginia Opioid Abatement Authority 2025 Annual Report
